coredata.dc:title: Contrasting spectroscopic response of human hemoglobin in presence of graphene oxides and its reduced form: Comparative approach with carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Recently, a considerable amount of research is being directed towards study of graphene oxide (GO) and its reduced form (RGO) since their exposed functional groups make them better candidates in nanobiotechnolgy. In order to assess their biocompatibility, the nature of interactions between Human Hemoglobin (HHb) and GO/RGO are monitored since a comparative spectroscopic approach towards understanding their nature of interactions has not been investigated previously. UV–vis spectroscopy reveals hyperchromicity for HHb-GO system and hypochromicity for HHb-RGO system in the region of absorption of tryptophan/tyrosine residues. Notably, although steady-state fluorescence static quenching of HHb for GO and enhancement of fluorescence for RGO is noticed, but average fluorescence-lifetime is remaining unchanged in presence of GO/RGO. Calorimetric data illustrates three-site and five-site binding model to be the best-fit model for GO and RGO respectively. Also, synchronous fluorescence quenching corresponding to alterations in microenvironment of tryptophan/ tyrosine residues is observed only in presence of GO. Likewise FTIR spectroscopy elucidates involvement of both amide I and amide II bond of HHb backbone through H-bonding interaction only for GO. Furthermore RLS spectra demonstrate an increase and a decrease in signal for GO and RGO respectively. Surprisingly, secondary structure of HHb is maintained upon interaction with both GO/RGO, as revealed by CD spectroscopy, thus supporting their potential application in biological microenvironment. Thus it appears that the spectroscopic properties of HHb upon interaction with GO is altered upon its reduction to RGO. Furthermore the role of HHb as good candidate for bimolecular interaction has been highlighted.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, a considerable amount of research is being directed towards study of graphene oxide (GO) and its reduced form (RGO) since their exposed functional groups make them better candidates in nanobiotechnolgy. In order to assess their biocompatibility, the nature of interactions between Human Hemoglobin (HHb) and GO/RGO are monitored since a comparative spectroscopic approach towards understanding their nature of interactions has not been investigated previously. UV–vis spectroscopy reveals hyperchromicity for HHb-GO system and hypochromicity for HHb-RGO system in the region of absorption of tryptophan/tyrosine residues. Notably, although steady-state fluorescence static quenching of HHb for GO and enhancement of fluorescence for RGO is noticed, but average fluorescence-lifetime is remaining unchanged in presence of GO/RGO. Calorimetric data illustrates three-site and five-site binding model to be the best-fit model for GO and RGO respectively. Also, synchronous fluorescence quenching corresponding to alterations in microenvironment of tryptophan/ tyrosine residues is observed only in presence of GO. Likewise FTIR spectroscopy elucidates involvement of both amide I and amide II bond of HHb backbone through H-bonding interaction only for GO. Furthermore RLS spectra demonstrate an increase and a decrease in signal for GO and RGO respectively. Surprisingly, secondary structure of HHb is maintained upon interaction with both GO/RGO, as revealed by CD spectroscopy, thus supporting their potential application in biological microenvironment. Thus it appears that the spectroscopic properties of HHb upon interaction with GO is altered upon its reduction to RGO. Furthermore the role of HHb as good candidate for bimolecular interaction has been highlighted.

ce:section.ce:para: Of late a novel two-dimensional (2D) carbon nanomaterial of single-atom-thickness, GO, which is an oxidized derivative of graphene, has attracted huge attention. GO has shown promising role for its wide-range applications specifically in biological environment due to its remarkable physicochemical properties like two-dimension nanostructure, high surface area to volume ratio, strong mechanical property and biocompatibility [1–5]. As already known, the graphene family comprises members including RGO, GO, graphene sheets and layered graphenes [5,6]. Since GO is prepared from highly oxidized form of graphene using strong oxidizing agents, unique characteristic of GO is achieved mainly due to the small sp2 carbon domains which are enclosed by sp3 domains and the hydrophilic functional groups [7].As such, the functionality of GO gets enhanced due to the presence of rich oxygen-containing surface functional groups like epoxide, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Thus among all the forms mentioned above GO received intense consideration for biomedical application due to its improved conductivity and virtuous biocompatibility [2,3,5].As such the rapid functionalization conferred on graphene possibly creates a convincing platform in molecular drug delivery [8–10], development of biosensors [11], treatment of cancer [12], bio-imaging [13], biomolecular recognition, molecular medicine and disease diagnosis [3,14], tissue engineering [8] and others.

Nevertheless, a vital concern must be addressed before the extensive application of GO in biological microenvironment is widely practiced. This can be realized particularly considering its biocompatible properties, exclusively during its accumulation, storage or retention in blood at the time of the study [15]. Accordingly, for potential biological application, GO needs to interact with the biomolecules and particularly with the protein since proteins are not only the building blocks of the body but also possess enormous range of functions [16]. It must be mentioned here that protein-nanoparticle interaction occurs predominantly by involving protein's backbone secondary structure and functional groups that may be exposed under physiological condition or that have got exposed due to conformational changes within protein upon interaction with nanoparticles. On the other hand the surface charge density of functional groups (mainly COOH and OH) on GO as determined during its preparation and storage probably allows its interaction with biomolecules like protein. The study of such interactions must have significant importance since there are possibilities of non-specific interaction of GO with biomolecules upon its exposure to biological microenvironment. Therefore substantive understanding of the interactions between nanoparticles and biological proteins is of immense importance for implementation of GO in biological microenvironment. Under these circumstances the use of proteins for investigating the nature of interaction with GO will also provide adequate knowledge in revealing the prospective role of GO in biological microenvironment. Moreover the reduced form of GO (RGO) has also been well studied as a carrier for imaging and drug delivery [17]. Reduction of GO to RGO modifies its compatibility, interaction properties and solubility in a biological system since the exposed functional groups on GO varies from those of RGO. Thus it becomes apparent that a comparative approach towards understanding of interaction of GO and RGO with proteins is of urgent requirement.

Accordingly human hemoglobin is chosen as a representative protein to elucidate the nature of interactions between HHb [18,19] and GO/RGO. It must be mentioned here that a comparative spectroscopic approach has not been undertaken previously in studying the nature of interactions of HHb with GO and RGO separately. As already known, that HHb maintains a constant supply of oxygen crucially vital for oxidative metabolism. HHb is a tetrameric protein of erythrocytes that contains heme, which is a cyclic tetrapyrrole connected by methylene bridges. Other substituents of heme include methyl, vinyl, and propionate groups. A central Fe2+ resides at the center of the planar tetrapyrrole and its oxidation to Fe3+destroys HHb's biological activity. Furthermore HHb is a tetramer consisted of pairs of two different polypeptide subunits - α and β, with a subunit composition of α2β2. The vital properties of HHb are dependent on its quaternary, tertiary as well its secondary structure [18,19].Importantly, each heme moiety binds one O2 and thus tetramericHHb binds four molecules of O2. Due to cooperative nature of O2 binding of HHb, one molecule of O2 binds to an HHb tetramer speedily if other O2 molecules are already attached. Upon interaction of the first O2 to deoxyHb shifting of a distance of 0.6 nm of the heme iron towards the plane of the heme ring occurs. As discussed next, such changes lead to substantive quaternary structural changes. The shift of heme iron further shifts the proximal (F8) histidine and to the neighboring residues, which thereby causes rupture of salt bridges between the carboxyl terminal residues of all four tetrameric subunits. Subsequently, rotation of 15 degrees of one pair of α/β subunits occurs and considerable changes in quaternary, tertiary and secondary structure thereby follows [18–20]. Thus it gives the impression that binding of small molecules, to HHb may generate noticeable changes in the overall quaternary, tertiary and secondary structure of the HHb and all such changes are expected to get detected spectroscopically at least to some extent. Under such circumstances it may happen that the physiologically significant protein may correspondingly interact with medically essential ligands other than oxygen for the purpose of development of drug-delivery system, biosensors and others[21]. The resulting structural changes due to interactions with small molecules are also expected to get detected through spectroscopic investigation. Consequently it is also understandable that unraveling the nature of such interactions can provide sufficient knowledge regarding the use of GO and RGO in the fields of nanobiochemistry, drug-delivery, biosensing, bio-imaging, medicinal chemistry, protein chemistry and others [22–24].

Therefore, the focus of our present investigation is to explore the interactions of GO and RGO with biologically significant protein HHb [18,19]. Primarily the impacts of GO on the spectroscopic and calorimetric properties of HHb are investigated. Notably the effects of reduction of GO on the spectroscopic and calorimetric properties of HHb are also elucidated. In most of the cases HHb appears to respond differently upon interaction with GO and RGO. The systematic efforts will provide substantial information regarding the potential biomedical use of GO and RGO in the biological milieu thereby integrating biological chemistry at the molecular level with GO and RGO. Also the role of HHb as model protein for bi- as well as biomolecular interaction will be obtained.

Human Hemoglobin (HHb), supplied by Sigma Aldrich is freshly prepared in double distilled and then deionized using a Millipore Milli-Q system water and concentration range of ~10−5- 10−6 M have been used for experimental purpose.

2 g of graphite powder is taken and mixed with 1 g of NaNO3 in an ice bath. Under constant stirring condition concentrated H2SO4 (130 ml) is carefully added to the mixture. Thereafter, 6 g of KMnO4 is added slowly through vigorous stirring but by maintaining the temperature of the reaction mixture at ~20 °C. The temperature of the reaction mixture is gradually elevated up to 40 °C for 6 h under stirring condition, when the color of the mixture is observed to change from dark grey to grayish green. Again additional 6 g of KMnO4 is added to the mixture, under stirring conditions for further 6 h as a result of which the color of the reaction mixture turns grayish brown. Then 250 ml of triple distilled water is carefully and slowly added to the solution and stirred for 30 min during which the temperature of the solution rises to ~96 °C. Thereafter the solution is allowed to cool to room temperature. Furthermore additional 500 ml triple distilled water and 15 ml 30% H2O2 are added to the reaction mixture to terminate the process of oxidation and at this stage, the color of the solution turns yellow ochre thereby indicating high oxidation level of graphite. The yellow solution is then washed twice with 1 M HCl solution and then repeated washing is carried out with triple distilled water to attain a solution of pH ~ 5. Thorough washing is performed by centrifugation of the solution and decantation of the supernatant. Vigorous washing and decantation is essentially required in order to exfoliate the graphene oxide layers and also removal of the unexfoliatedgraphene oxide layers. The resulting thick yellow brown gel is filtered and kept overnight for allowing it to dry to obtain a fine yellow graphene oxide (GO) powder. ~2 mg of GO is then taken in triple distilled water and ultrasonicated for 30 min to get a homogeneous solution [25,26].

In order to prepare RGO, hydrazine hydrate (2 ml) is added to the solution. The reaction mixture is then heated to 100 °C for 6 h to obtain black RGO. Then the black solid is washed and the filtrate is dried at 80 °C to get RGO. To get a uniform dispersion ~2 mg of RGO is taken in triple distilled water and ultrasonicated for ~90 min.The preparations of both GO and RGO and their characterizations were made according to the methodology described elsewhere [25,26].
All the reagents required in the present study are purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used in the experiments without any further steps of purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of GO and RGO
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of pure graphite, GO and RGO were recorded using a Bruker X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (model: D8 Advance DAVINCI). The XRD patterns were analyzed to determine the crystallographic information of the samples. Electron microscopic study of GO was carried out for morphological analysis using a JEOL JEM-2010 High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) and a Zeiss Sigma Field Emission Scanning electron microscope (FESEM).
Dilute solutions of the HHb (~10−5–10−6 M) are taken for the following techniques in absence and presence of GO/ RGO at the ambient temperature [18,19,27].

UV–vis absorption of HHb are measured in absence and presence of GO and RGO in rectangular quartz cell of 1 cm path length at the ambient temperature. UV–vis absorption spectra are scanned by using JASCO UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometer (Model: V-630).

Steady state fluorescence emission spectra of HHb are measured in absence and presence of GO and RGO in rectangular quartz cell of 1 cm path length at the ambient temperature using JASCO Spectrofluorimeter (Model: 8200).

RLS spectra of HHb are measured in absence and presence of GO and RGO in rectangular quartz cell of 1 cm path length at the ambient temperature using JASCO Spectrofluorimeter (Model: 8200) by setting ∆λ = 0 nm.

Synchronous fluorescence spectra of HHb are measured in absence and presence of GO and RGO in rectangular quartz cells of 1 cm path length at the ambient temperature. Synchronous fluorescence spectra are monitored using JASCO Spectrofluorimeter (Model: 8200) by adjusting ∆λ = 15 nm for tyrosine residues and ∆λ = 60 nm for tryptophan residues.

Time resolved spectroscopic measurements for fluorescence lifetime determinations of HHb in absence and presence of GO and RGO are carried out by employing a time correlated single-photon-counting (TCSPC) technique using the model FLUOROLOG TCSPC HORIBA JOBIN YVON at 280 nm (NanoLED 280) as impulse for excitation in Trp/Tyr region and an emission wavelength set at 330 nm. The decay kinetics, the quality of fit with a plot of weighted residuals and other statistical parameters like reduced χ 2 and the Durbin–Watson (DW) parameters are checked for the entire decay along with the rising edge.

Titrations are carried out in Isothermal Titration Calorimeter (ITC 200). Briefly the titrations comprise of 20 injections of GO. Injection of 2 μl aliquots are set to pass into a stirred reaction cell (volume- 200 μl) enclosing HHb. The temperature is maintained at 298 K, reference power is set at10 μcal/s and the stirring rate is adjusted to 300 rpm. Base-line correction is executed to each experiment by subtraction of data from a series of injections of GO/RGO into water (Millipore Milli-Q system) as blank. Then the blank data is subtracted. Thereafter the nonlinear least squares method is applied to minimize χ2 values and correspondingly the best fit parameters are evaluated. Considering the interactions of HHb and GO/RGO, the sequential-binding site model appears to possess the best-fit parameters. After that the titration curves are assessed with the sequential-binding model using the analysis software (Origin 7.0) fitted in the attached model.
FTIR spectra are monitored in Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer by means of CaF2 cell.
CD spectra are recorded using JASCO CD spectropolarimeter (Model J-815) in a rectangular quartz cell (1 mm), at 25 °C, within the spectral region of 250–190 nm.

The following observations are collected in triplicates and then plotted. Analysis of the plots is performed using Microcal Origin (version 8.5) software after gathering original raw data directly from the apparatus used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: To check the UV–vis absorption spectroscopic properties of HHb in presence of GO/RGO
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly GO and RGO (after preparation)have been characterized using XRD, HRTEM and FESEM techniques before assessing their nature of interactions with HHb. The XRD patterns of the graphite, GO and RGO are shown in Fig. 1A. In the case of pure graphite, XRD pattern reveals that the characteristic (002) peak appears at 2θ = 26.51° and other peak with relatively weak intensity appears at 2θ = 44.47° and 2θ = 54.01°, corresponding to the (101) and (004) plane of graphite (JCPDS file no. 65–6212). The XRD pattern of the GO sample synthesized from the graphite exhibits the maximum peak (002) at 2θ = 9.46° and for RGO a typical broad peak corresponding (002) appears at 2θ = 24.86° and a weak peak at 2θ = 43.01°. Comparing the XRD diffraction patterns of the samples it is observed that reduction process can be used to restore crystalline structure. No other satellite peak appear in the powder XRD pattern of Graphite, GO and RGO which justifies the absence of impurities in the sample. HRTEM (Fig. 1B) and FESEM micrograph (Fig. 1C) of GO exhibit sheet like morphology with thick stacking of layers.

Then as a first step to reveal the spectroscopic properties of HHb in presence of GO and RGO, UV–vis electronic absorption spectroscopy is performed. The UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb in Millipore water under physiological condition (pH ~ 7.0) upon addition of increasing concentration of GO is shown in Fig. 2A. From Fig. 2A it is observed that the absorption spectra of HHb possess a broad absorption peak at ~270 nm which corresponds primarily due to the presence of tryptophan and tyrosine. Upon addition of increasing concentration of GO to HHb solution, hyperchromicity of this peak without any noticeable spectral shift is observed.

Absorption spectroscopic measurements of HHb are then monitored in presence of RGO. Here also the absorption spectra of HHb (Fig. 2B) show a broad absorption peak at around ~270 nm. Upon addition of increasing concentration of RGO the entire absorption spectra display a minor yet noticeable hypochromic effect without any visible spectral shift.

Furthermore the absorption spectra of GO displays a broad absorption maxima at 229 nm due to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and a shoulder at around ~301 nm due to the n-π* transition of CO bonds (Fig. 2C). Absorption spectra of RGO further reveal broad absorption maxima at 263 nm (Fig. 2D). Thus it appears that the λmax has shifted from 229 nm for GO to 263 nm for RGO thus indicating the effective reduction of the GO by hydrazine hydrate [25,26,28]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of GO further suggests that the highest concentrations of GO that is used in this study, show absorbance below ~0.07 at around 270 nm (Fig. 2C). Likewise UV–vis absorption spectra of RGO indicates that the highest concentrations of RGO, display negligible absorbance below ~0.02 at around 270 nm (Fig. 2D). Since the absorbance of GO or RGO at the region of absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine residues (270 nm) is much less, excitation at this wavelength will primarily generate fluorescence emission of the protein and not the graphenes.

The absorption spectra of HHb also exhibit an intense Soret band peak at around ~405 nm which is attributed to the presence of porphyrin ring in HHb [19,29,30] (Fig. 2A and B). The Soret bands are known to occur due to electronic transitions (π-π⁎) from ground state or S0 to the second excited state S2 within the hematoporphyrin that remain embedded in a hydrophobic pocket formed by three dimensional folding of the multi-subunit protein. The nature of Soret band as monitored by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy display an increase in intensity upon gradual addition of GO thus indicating alteration in the microenvironment surrounding HHb possibly due to perturbation around heme group [31–33]. Also involvement of heme group during formation of ground-state complex in presence of GO must be taken into account. Interestingly the Soret band intensity of HHb nearly remains unchanged in presence of RGO compared to GO thereby suggesting reduced changes within heme environment upon interaction with reduced form of GO (RGO). Accordingly it appears that the hyperchromic effect of the Soret band of HHb in presence of GO is lessened upon its reduction to RGO.

Overall although the above observations as obtained from UV–vis absorption spectroscopic studies demonstrate the hyperchromic effect for GO and the hypochromic effect for RGO in the region of absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine residues, however in spite of these interactions the overall absorption spectral properties is not much affected. Also it appears that the microenvironment of aromatic amino acid residues and heme group are involved in the formation of ground state complexes for HHb-GO system. Mostly the alterations in absorption spectral properties are observed to be even lesser in presence of RGO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alterations in steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence properties of HHb in presence of GO/RGO
ce:section.ce:para: It is already well known that a variety of molecular interactions like formation of ground-state complex can alter fluorescence properties of proteins [18,21,34–37]. This is because the fluorescence spectra of the protein (HHb) usually determine the microenvironment of the fluorescing aromatic residues (tryptophan and tyrosine residues) and thus any change in their neighboring environment will probably get revealed by monitoring the variations in the spectra. Thus to unravel the microenvironmental changes around tryptophan and tyrosine residues, fluorescence spectral measurements are carried out in presence of GO and RGO. The steady state fluorescence emission spectra of HHb as shown in Fig. 3A reveal an emission maximum at ~331 nm when excited at ~270 nm. Fig. 3A shows that with increase in concentration of GO, the fluorescence maxima of HHb is observed to quench regularly without any noticeable spectral shift. Fluorescence quenching is also monitored at different temperatures: 288 K, 298 K, 308 K and 318 K [18,21,34,35].

The alteration in steady state fluorescence emission spectra of HHb in presence of RGO at the ambient temperature has been represented in the Fig. 3B. Here also emission maximum at ~331 nm when excited at ~270 nm has been observed. Interestingly Fig. 3B shows that upon addition of increasing concentration of RGO, there is no quenching of fluorescence. Instead, enhancement of fluorescence without any spectral shift is noticed. Thus the quenching effect of GO got abolished with a concomitant fluorescence enhancement effect upon its reduction to RGO. Such observations can be explained at least to some extent from the fact that fluorescence occurs due to two processes: firstly it occurs due to excitation by the incident field determined by the local environment, and secondly due to emission of radiation determined by the balance of radiative and non-radiative decay. While the source of the first process is the external radiation field, in the second process it is the molecule itself which comprises the source [37]. In the present study the decrease and increase in fluorescence intensity may perhaps occur due to changes in microenvironment around tryptophan and tyrosine residues. Further investigations are needed in order to explore other possibilities. However it can be realized that in spite of the changes in the intensity of fluorescence upon interaction with GO and RGO the polarity around tryptophan and tyrosine residues are hardly affected probably due to absence of any spectral shift.

The fluorescence quenching of HHb in case of interaction with GO, are then studied by using Stern-Volmer (SV) relation
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q
where F 0 and F indicates the fluorescence intensities of HHb in absence and presence of GO. K
SV refers to the Stern-Volmer (SV) constant and [Q] denotes the concentrations of the GO used. The representative SV plot for the HHb-GO complex appears to be linear thus indicating that the quenching type can either be of pure dynamic or of pure static in nature (Fig. 4A). In order to distinguish whether the type of quenching is static or dynamic in nature, temperature dependence of K
SV (Fig. 4B) and fluorescence lifetime measurements are then performed (Fig. 5 ). It is already known that for static quenching, the value of K
SV decreases whereas for dynamic quenching the value of K
SV increases with increase of temperature. This is because an increase of temperature would result in an increase in rate of effective collision between the reacting species through diffusion mechanisms thereby enabling the dynamic energy transfer process to occur. In the present case, the value of K
SV appear to decrease with increase in temperature for HHb-GO interactions (Table 1 ). Thus the type of quenching of fluorescence of HHb by GO should primarily be of static in nature. The SV equation could not be applied for HHb-RGO system since there is no fluorescence quenching.

In order to validate the possibility of static quenching processes, fluorescence lifetimes are also determined by time resolved spectroscopic measurements; using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) techniques (Fig. 5). Upon excitation at 280 nm three lifetimes are observed- 0.19 ns, 1.06 ns and 3.27 ns (Table 2 ) with their corresponding fractional contributions in case of HHb. The lifetime components are 0.11 ns, 0.79 ns and 2.97 ns in presence of GO and 0.18 ns, 0.95 ns and 3.19 ns in presence of RGO respectively. Here also the fractional contribution for each component remains nearly unchanged in presence of GO/RGO. Thus it is apparent that the lifetime of the individual components of HHb do not change significantly. For further understanding, the average emission lifetime, <τ > is calculated using the following equation
(2)
τ
=
∑
a i
τ i
2
∑
a i
τ i
where a
i and τ
i signify normalized pre-exponential factor and corresponding lifetime respectively. As evident in Table 2, the average lifetime 〈τ〉 for HHb appears to be 1.5 ns. Moreover the lifetimes of HHb do not change significantly upon addition of GO and RGO (τ for HHb-GO is 1.3 ns and for HHb-RGO is 1.5 ns). The nearly unchanged values of τ of HHb even in presence of GO/RGO additionally suggest the type of quenching to be static in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ITC analysis to assess the nature of interaction between HHb and GO/RGO
ce:section.ce:para: From the above observations it appears that the functional groups of GO/RGO are interacting with HHb and thereby altering the microenvironment around tryptophan and tyrosine residues. In order to assess the thermodynamic features of interacting forces between HHb and GO/RGO, ITC is performed. The calorimetric summary of the titration of GO and RGO with HHb has been displayed in Fig. 6 . The heat burst curves are represented in the figure after correcting it by subtraction of the respective heat of dilution acquired from control experiments (injection of GO or RGO into the water (Millipore Milli-Q system) only. The values obtained after correction are plotted along with molar ratio. The lower panels in Fig. 6 indicate heat changes that are best fitted by a sequential binding model.

In case of interaction with GO, the isotherm profile of heat changes versus molar ratio of GO for HHb is best fitted (least χ2 values) to a three-site sequential binding model (Fig. 6A). As evident in the figure, the binding appears to be an exothermic process. Accordingly the integrated heats of injection display a tendency towards initial increasingly negative enthalpy, followed by a decreasing negative enthalpy. Evaluation of thermodynamic parameters reveals the following observations summarized in Table 3A . The binding constant for the first-site sequential binding (K1) appears to be 1.58 × 105 M−1. As observed in the table, the binding constant appears to decrease (K2–1.09 × 104 M−1) for the second sequential binding and then again increased for the third site sequential binding (K3–9.35 × 104 M−1). Moreover the binding is driven by negative enthalpic change for first sequential binding and a highly negative enthalpy change for second binding site. But for the third-site sequential binding, ∆H appears to be positive also in the order of 104. Furthermore the entropy change signifies positive value for first binding site and higher positive value for third sequential binding. Whereas a negative value for ∆S is noticed for second binding site. As such the binding appears predominantly to be enthalpy driven for the three-site sequential binding of HHb and GO. However a steady increase in magnitude of ∆S also implies that these bindings are both enthalpy and entropy driven, enthalpy changes being the dominant contributor to ∆G. The gradual increase in magnitude of ∆S also indicates conformational alteration within the protein microenvironment in presence of GO. Furthermore the values of ∆G are determined from the following well-known Gibbs–Helmholtz equation (Eq. (3)).
(3)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ S
It must be mentioned here that ∆G < 0, in all cases, thereby suggesting, spontaneity of interactions for all three-site sequential binding, upon interaction with GO.

So far, evaluation of thermodynamic parameters suggests that the interactions for the first binding site are possibly electrostatic in nature with highest binding constant K1 of value- 1.58 × 105 M−1. The electrostatic interactions (∆H < 0 and ∆S > 0) are probably occurring by involving charged residues of HHb upon interaction with GO for the first binding site. These interactions are probably causing microenvironmental alterations thereby by exposing polar uncharged residues for the formation of hydrogen bond and van der Waals interaction(∆H < 0 and ∆S < 0) for the second sequential binding. These interactions may involve the indole N of tryptophan residues and OH group of tyrosine residues by formation of hydrogen bond and van der Waals interaction. The interpretations are supported by our observations from synchronous and FTIR spectroscopy experiments as discussed in the following sections. The third-site sequential binding is determined by hydrophobic interactions (∆H > 0 and ∆S > 0). This may be expected since the interactions of HHb with GO are probably causing certain conformational alteration thereby exposing the hydrophobic residues of the globular protein HHb for interaction with GO. This interpretation is supported by the observation that the third-site sequential binding shows highest positive entropy value (∆S = 322 cal/mol/K).

Evaluation of thermodynamic parameters of interaction between HHb and RGO are then probed (Fig. 6B). Thermodynamic parameters as obtained from the ITC analysis reveal five-site sequential binding to be the best-fit model (Table 3B ). Here also the binding appears to be an exothermic process with decreasing negative enthalpy. Interestingly the binding constants remain nearly identical for first, third and fifth sequential binding (K`1 = 1.11 × 105 M−1, K`3 = 1.1 × 105 M−1, K`5 = 1.05 × 105 M−1). Although the binding constants for second (K`2) and fourth (K`4) sequential binding also gets decreased but interestingly the values of K2 and K4 are remaining nearly unchanged (K`2 = 9.38 × 104, K`4 = 9.31 × 104). As evident in Table 3B, negative values of ∆H are observed for first, second and fourth sequential binding. A gradual increase in positive enthalpy change is observed for third and fifth sequential binding. Moreover negative entropy values are also observed for first, second and fourth binding site. Positive entropy values with a gradual increase in magnitude are observed for third and fifth site sequential binding. Also comparatively higher enthalpy values at all the five sites suggest that the binding is driven by enthalpic changes. However since the values of entropy are higher for fourth and fifth binding site, these bindings appear to be both enthalpy and entropy driven. Higher positive values of entropy further suggest structural alterations within protein microenvironment upon interaction with RGO. Also the changes in ∆S appear to be more upon interaction of HHb with RGO compared to GO. However the binding constant (K1) appears to be highest for first-site sequential binding in presence of GO.

Then the nature of interactions between HHb and RGO are revealed. As evident in Table 3B, hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interaction, (∆H < 0 and ∆S < 0) is observed for first, second and fourth site sequential binding. The third and fifth site binding appears to be hydrophobic in nature (∆H > 0 and ∆S > 0). Furthermore the values of ∆G, as determined from the above-mentioned Gibbs–Helmholtz equation (Table 3B) appear to be negative for all five-site sequential binding thus signifying spontaneity of the interactions between HHb and RGO. Moreover the binding constant for the first site sequential binding (K`1 = 1.11 × 105 M−1) is highest. These observations are going in favor with the interpretations obtained from steady-state and synchronous spectroscopy (next section), which will reveal alterations in microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues of HHb upon interaction with GO/RGO. Precisely the indoleN of tryptophan and OH group of tyrosine may get involved in hydrogen bonding interactions with HHb's backbone and such interpretation needs further elucidation. Also such interactions are causing certain structural orientation within HHb that may lead to alterations in the microenvironment of nonpolar residues for the formation of hydrophobic interactions.Furthermore as already mentioned that electrostatic interactions appear to be the mode of interaction between HHb and GO with highest binding constant (K1) for the first site sequential binding. But interestingly the electrostatic mode of interactions do not appear to occur upon reduction of GO to RGO with HHb.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elucidating the alterations in microenvironment of HHb by synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: As a next step, synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy is performed to monitor the effect of weak non-covalent interactions (especially H-bonding and van der Waals interactions) between HHb and GO/RGO particularly involving tryptophan and tyrosine and its neighboring residueswithin HHb's microenvironment. As already known, it is a well acquainted method that deals with scanning of the excitation and emission monochromators simultaneously during which a constant wavelength interval (∆λ) is maintained between them. When ∆λ is set at15 nm synchronous fluorescence indicates alterations in microenvironment of tyrosine residues and ∆λ is set at 60 nm, the synchronous fluorescence specifies changes in microenvironment around tryptophan residues [18,38,39]. Also any shift noticed in the emission maximum indicates variation in polarity around the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues.

Fig. 7A and C displays synchronous fluorescence spectra of HHb in presence of increasing concentration of GO at ∆λ = 15 nm (Fig. 7A) and ∆λ = 60 nm (Fig. 7C) respectively. The synchronous fluorescence intensity appears to steadily decrease for the tyrosine (∆λ = 15 nm) peak upon gradual increase in the concentration of GO. As evident in Fig. 7A, the synchronous fluorescence spectra for (∆λ = 15 nm), exhibited emission maximum at 306 nm with no spectral shift upon addition of GO. Although the microenvironment around tyrosine residues is affected but the polarity around the tyrosine residues appears to remain more or less unperturbed in presence of GO due to absence of any spectral shift. Furthermore synchronous fluorescence spectra of HHb for the tryptophan peaks (∆λ = 60 nm) shows an emission maxima at ~337 nm (Fig. 7C). Upon gradual addition of GO the synchronous fluorescence intensity appears to gradually decrease with no noticeable spectral shift thereby signifying alterations around microenvironment of tryptophan residues. It must be mentioned here that alterations in the synchronous fluorescence intensity may involve hydrogen bonding interaction involving hydroxyl group of tyrosine and indole N of tryptophan residues probably due to structural alterations within microenvironment of HHb and its backbone conformation in presence of GO.Due to absence of any spectral shift, the polarity around tryptophan residues also remains unaltered.

However in presence of RGO, the synchronous fluorescence intensity of HHb does not exhibit any significant change, for both tyrosine (emission maxima at 306 nm) (Fig. 7B) and tryptophan residues (emission maxima at 337 nm) (Fig. 7D). The observations probably imply that the microenvironment of both the tyrosine and tryptophan residues nearly remains unaffected in presence of RGO in spite of formation of weak non-covalent interaction between HHb and RGO as evident in ITC analysis.

Thus it appears that upon interaction with GO the weak non-covalent forces are formed involving hydroxyl group of tyrosine and indole group of tryptophan residues. Whether such interactions are also affecting the hydrogen bonding pattern of polypeptide backbone needs to be revealed (discussed in the following sections). Intriguingly such interactions are not promoting any change in the polarity around the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues of HHb. Altogether both GO and RGO have not induced any significant structural modification within HHb that may affect the physiological function of the protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interpreting the modes of interaction involving backbone of HHb by using FT-IR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: In order to check how the weak non-covalent forces particularly, hydrogen bonding interactions are involving the polypeptide backbone during interactions with GO/ RGO, FT-IR spectroscopy is then performed. It must be kept in mind that FT-IR bands of proteins responds to secondary structural alterations related to interactions involving protein's backbone [18,19,40,41]. Precisely FT-IR measurements are monitored aiming on carbonyl (C=O) stretching mode of HHb upon interaction with GO/RGO. As evident in Fig. 8 , a prominent absorption band in the region (1700–1600 cm−1, amide I band) related to CO and CN stretching mode of peptide linkages within HHb is noticed at 1650 cm−1. The amide I band intensity decreases with broadening of the peak but spectral shift is not observed in presence of GO (Fig. 8A). The amide II band (amide II region- 1620–1500 cm−1) of HHb resides at 1546 cm−1 region and it is known to arise from the in-plain NH bending and stretching of CN. This amide II band gets broadened and a decrease in IR absorption intensity along with 1 nm red shift is noticed upon interaction with GO. These changes in the IR absorption intensity of the CO and NH stretching vibration might suggest the participation of the CO as well as NH group of the polypeptide chain probably in the formation of hydrogen bond by involving both the hydroxyl group present within tyrosine moiety as well as the indole N atom of tryptophan residues. In other words alteration within protein microenvironment particularly around tryptophan and tyrosine residues upon interaction with GO and RGO have again beenindicated.

Accordingly in case of interactions of HHb with RGO the amide I and amide II band of HHb nearly remain unchanged (Fig. 8B). The observations indicate that probably there is very less possibility of hydrogen bonding interaction involving CO and NH group of the polypeptide backbone. Hydrogen bonding interaction involving other polar amino acids may also possibly occur.

Therefore the observations reveal comparable nature of the FT-IR bands of HHb upon interaction with GO and RGO. However maintenance of essential features of secondary structure of HHb even in presence of GO and RGO respectively is apparent from the observations. Furthermore the probable interactions could as well comprise of other weak non-covalent interactions like electrostatic (only for GO) and hydrophilic and also hydrophobic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Illustrating the secondary structural changes of HHb using CD spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to gain further knowledge regarding the effect of hydrogen bonding interactions within the polypeptide backbone (secondary structure) CD spectroscopy is performed. CD spectra of HHb will precisely determine its overall secondary structural alterations upon interactions with GO/ RGO [42]. Fig. 9 ,displays negative bands at about 209 and 220 nm that occurs due to presence of α-helices within HHb thereby providing information regarding HHb's secondary structure. The intensities of the negative bands at 209 nm although increases slightly upon addition of GO but the band at 220 nm remains almost unchanged (Fig. 9A). However in presence of RGO, the intensities of the negative bands at 209 and 220 nm decrease marginally (Fig. 9B). For further clarification, the CD results are then expressed in terms of mean residue ellipticity (MRE) in degree cm2 mol−1 according to the following equation
(4)
θ λ
=
θ
10 nlc
where c denotes the concentration of protein in g/ml, θ determines the observed rotation in degree, l indicates the path length in cm, and n signifies the number of amino acid residues (574) of HHb. Estimation of the percentage of α-helix within HHb in presence of GO/RGO has been quantified using the given equations
(5)
% ofα − helix =

Also the highest binding constant (K1) as noticed in ITC analysisis observed for electrostatic interaction of HHb and GO layers. The polar charged residues of HHb involved in the electrostatic interaction are usually exposed to interact during bimolecular interactions. Hydrogen bonding, van der Waals interaction, hydrophobic interactions although are observed for second and third sequential binding but these interactions did not affect the polypeptide backbone. Minimal effect on polypeptide backbone is detected by FT-IR spectroscopy. Since secondary structural alterations are largely dependent on conformational alteration of polypeptide backbone thus if polypeptide backbone is not much affected the overall secondary structure will also remain unaltered. This observation has been confirmed by CD spectral analysis. Also from the above studies it appears that for a multi-subunit protein like HHb, it may happen that the GO sheets are probably and primarily accessing the surface of its interacting partner HHb.

Moreover for RGO the modes of interaction for five site sequential binding observed from ITC analysis appear to be hydrogen bonding, van der Waals interaction and hydrophobic interaction. However as observed from FT-IR spectroscopy that in spite of the above interactions the polypeptide backbone involving the amide linkage is not much involved in interactions with RGO layers.Here also secondary structural alterations are mostly determined by conformational alterations of polypeptide backbone. If the polypeptide backbone remains almost unaltered the overall secondary structure will also remain unaffected. This observation has been confirmed by CD spectral analysis. Apparently for a multi-subunit protein like HHb, probably the RGO sheets are getting major access to the surface of its interacting partner HHb.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unraveling the effect of aggregation by resonance light scattering (RLS) spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: In order to check whether the interactions between HHb and GO/RGO are having any effect of aggregation, RLS spectroscopy is performed. By using a fluorimeter in synchronous wavelength scanning mode with ∆λ = 0 nm the corresponding RLS spectra are recorded from 250 to 650 nm [18,19,25,43,44].

Upon addition of GO to HHb solution, a gradual increase in RLS signal is noticed (Fig. 10A). It must be mentioned here that an increase in RLS signal indicates formation of HHb-GO complex with increased particle dimension that may lead to formation of aggregate in the solution.

Interestingly decrease in RLS signal of HHb is observed upon gradual addition of RGO (Fig. 10B). Since the RLS intensity is contributed mostly due to formation of aggregates in solution, therefore the observations possibly suggest that there is occurrence of no such aggregation effect. This may be expected since hydrogen bond formation involving polypeptide backbone as evident in the FT-IR (Fig. 8B) and CD (Fig. 9B) spectra is observed to possess minimal changes upon interaction with RGO. Thus the possibility of formation of aggregates appears to be more upon interaction with GO compared to RGO. Furthermore the weak binding forces between HHb and GO are probably facilitating such complex formation. Here electrostatic interaction between HHb and GO may play dominant role in complex formation. This may be realized since electrostatic interaction and aggregation via complex formation has not occurred upon interaction with RGO.

Therefore from the above systematic efforts it seems that GO and RGO can have wide-range of biological application due to its exposed functional groups particularly in the fields of development of drug-delivery system, biosensors, nanobiochemistry, protein chemistry and others. In this respect HHb has also acted as a good candidate in studying the nature of interactions between GO and RGO. As such the role of HHb as model protein, for studying bimolecular interactions with other members of carbon nanofamily, has been discussed in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of interactions of hemoglobin with other members of carbon nanofamily
ce:section.ce:para: Earlier we checked the microenvironmental alterations of HHb in presence of carbon quantum dots (CQD), nitrogen doped quantum dots (NCQD) [19]. In relation to this in the present section the nature of interactions of HHb with different members of carbon nanofamily has been summarized (Table 5 ). The knowledge regarding the modes of interaction of HHb with members of carbon nanofamily – CQD, NCQD, GO and RGO will help in realizing the potential role of carbon nanoforms for biological applications especially in the development of drug-delivery systems, biosensors, nanobiochemistry, protein biochemistry and others.Also individual role of members of carbon nanofamily as a better candidate for biomedical application can be obtained.Essentially, the role of HHb as model protein in studying bimolecular interaction will also be understood.

Precisely UV–vis absorption spectroscopyof HHbunravelhyperchomicity in the region of absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine residuesin presence of GO. Whereas hypochromicity is noticed in that region upon reduction of GO to RGO. However upon interaction with CQD and NCQD hyperchromicity is observed in both the cases. The Soret band at ~405 nm shows hyperchromicity in presence of GO and remaining nearly unchanged in presence of RGO. Whereas hypochromicity of Soret band is, noticed in presence of CQD and NCQD. The observations are interesting since HHb alone is interacting with members of carbon nanofamily in varied ways.

Secondly, steady-state fluorescence properties of HHb show quenching in presence of GO and enhancement in fluorescence intensity in presence of RGO but with no spectral shift in both the cases. Thus the effect of GO on fluorescence properties of HHb is reversed upon its reduction to RGO. Also fluorescence enhancement with 10 nm red shift is observed upon interaction with both CQD and NCQD. However similar observation is witnessed during estimation of average fluorescence life time (τ) of HHb in presence of GO and RGO, that is τ remaining unchanged in presence of both GO and RGO. However an increase in average lifetime of HHbup to ~1.7 times isrecordedupon interaction with both CQD and NCQD. Therefore steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence of HHb alters in a different way in presence of GO/RGO and CQD/NCQD.

In case of synchronous fluorescence intensity changes of HHbat ∆λ = 15 nm, quenching with no spectral shift is noticed upon interaction with GO. These changes in synchronous fluorescence spectral properties is not observed upon reduction of GO to RGO. Similar observations are observed for synchronous fluorescence spectral properties at ∆λ = 60 nm, upon interaction with GO/RGO (fluorescence quenching in presence of GO, no change in fluorescence properties in presence of RGO). But fluorescence enhancement with 7 nm blue shift is observed in presence of CQD/NCQDat ∆λ = 15 nm. However fluorescence enhancement with 2 nm red shift is observed in presence of CQD/NCQDat ∆λ = 60 nm. Thus synchronous fluorescence properties of HHb vary distinctly for members of carbon nanofamily.

ITC studies also reveal three-site sequential binding to be the best-fit model for HHb-GO system and a five-site sequential binding to be the best-fit model for HHb-RGO system. Furthermore, five-site sequential model appears to be the best-fit model for interaction of HHbwith both CQD/NCQD. Thus the nature of binding of HHb turns up to be different for members of graphenes and carbon quantum dots.

Moreover the polypeptide backbone conformation of HHb preferably through H-bonding interaction also variesin a dissimilar manner as detected by FT-IR spectroscopy. FT-IR spectra for amide I band intensity of HHb is decreased in presence of GO and nearly remains unchanged upon its reduction to RGO. Decrease in amide I band intensity is also noticed for HHb upon interaction with both QDs separately.

Furthermore alterations in the effect of aggregation due to complex formation with HHb are also evident in the RLS spectra. As such the RLS intensity of HHb although increases in presence of GO but a decrease in the RLS intensity is detected upon its reduction to RGO. Also an increase in RLS intensity is sightedin presence of CQD, but the RLS band remainsunaffected in presence of NCQD.

However in spite of all the above changes in HHb, the overall secondary structure is preserved and its integrity maintained upon interaction with all the members of carbon nanofamily. These observations suggest potential application of the nanoparticles in biological microenvironment, especially as drug-delivery system, biosensors and other notable biochemical applications including protein biochemistry, nanobiochemistry [45–49]. Also the possible role of clinically significant HHb [50] as a model protein for bimolecular interaction with different nanoparticles has been illustrated. Importantly the observations further reveal that the members of graphene family are better candidates for biomedical application compared to carbon quantum dot family. As evident in Table 5, the alterations in spectroscopic properties of HHb in almost all cases appears to occur to a greater extent upon interaction with carbon quantum dots compared to graphene oxides [18]. These effects are even more prominent for steady-state, time resolved and synchronous fluorescence spectral observations of carbon quantum dots compared to graphene oxides.A representative comparison method has been represented to validate the information provided in Table 5 (Table 6 ). Thus from Table 6 it becomes obvious that the changes in spectral properties of HHb in presence of CQD and NCQD occur in a greater extent than compared to the spectral changes upon interaction with GO and RGO. The enhanced alterations in spectroscopic properties of HHb in presence of CQD and NCQD are observed in almost all the spectroscopic techniques studied here. In other words the summary of the above experimental results suggests that graphene oxides are more biocompatible compared to carbon quantum dots for potential biomedical application at least when HHb is considered as the model protein for studying bimolecular interaction.

It must be mentionedhere that exposed functional groups of GO/RGO enable them with enhanced stability, biocompatibility within the biological surrounding. Also the alterations in spectroscopic properties of HHb are nearly opposing for GO and its reduced form RGO. The extent of reduction of GO thus becomes very important as performed here since nearly contrasting observations are noticed for GO and its reduced form RGO.All in all it appears that although HHbis interacting with members of carbon nanofamily in varied ways, however HHb possesses the ability to preserve its secondary structure in spite of all such interactions.

The systematic efforts elucidate the characteristic spectroscopic properties of HHb upon interaction with GO and RGO. Interestingly, HHb responds to GO and RGO in a noticeably different manner, at least, when the spectroscopic properties of HHb are concerned. GO is prepared by graphite exfoliation using strong oxidizing agents and RGO is synthesized by treating GO with hydrazine hydrate. As the resulting nanoforms formed have different functional groups exposed for interaction with HHb, these functional groups react specifically with HHb and thereby alter the protein microenvironment in their own way. Accordingly such alterations within protein microenvironment are evident from absorption spectral data particularly around the tryptophan and tyrosine residuesthat display hyperchromicity upon interaction with GO and a hypochoromicity upon interaction with RGO. The Soret band also responds differently in presence of GO/RGO. Moreover the microenvironment around the vicinity of the fluorophores also varies distinctly. Intriguingly in spite of the above alterations within the microenvironment of HHb upon interaction with GO and RGO the overall secondary structure of the protein is well preserved and its integrity is almostretained. Furthermore comparative spectroscopic analysis reveals graphene oxides as better candidate for biomedical application compared to carbon quantum dots. This can be realized since in most of the experimental observations the alterations in spectroscopic properties of Hemoglobin appears to occur in a lesser extent in presence of graphene oxides compared to carbon quantum dots. Therefore our work not only suggestsbutalso justifiespotential applications of GO and RGO in the biological system, especially as drug delivery system, delivery of small molecules and biosensors, nano-biochemistry, nano-medicine, nanomolecules and others. Moreover the differentlyexposed functional groups of GO and RGO support them with improved stability and interactive properties thereby rendering them more biocompatible within the biological microenvironment. Overall our investigation not only reveals the nature of interaction of HHb and GO/RGO but in a broader perspective also highlight the wide-range of application of GO and RGO as robust candidate for sophisticated biomedical applications.
